name: Water Panther
description: The Water Panther, also known as the Underwater Panther or Mishipeshu (Ojibwe), is a powerful and often dangerous creature from Native American mythology, particularly among tribes of the Great Lakes region and the Mississippi Valley. It is a composite being, typically depicted with the head and paws of a feline, often a panther or mountain lion, but with scales, horns, and occasionally the tail of a fish or serpent. It is a guardian of the underwater world and controls the flow of rivers and lakes. The Water Panther is often associated with copper, a valuable resource, and is seen as both a protector and a threat. Its image is frequently found on rock art, pottery, and other artifacts, indicating its significant role in the spiritual beliefs and practices of these cultures. While some tribes view it as a benevolent spirit that can bring good fortune and healing, others consider it a malevolent force responsible for storms, floods, and drownings. Respect and reverence are crucial when interacting with areas believed to be inhabited by the Water Panther.
subtitle: Guardian of the underwater realm.
appearance: The Water Panther is typically depicted as a large feline, resembling a panther or mountain lion, but with notable aquatic features. It often has scales covering parts of its body, horns on its head (sometimes deer-like or bison-like), and a long, serpentine tail, or the tail of a fish. Its fur can range in color from black or dark brown to vibrant shades of green or blue. Some depictions include sharp claws and teeth, emphasizing its predatory nature. The eyes are often described as glowing or possessing supernatural power.
habitat: The Water Panther resides primarily in deep lakes, rivers, and other large bodies of water. It is believed to inhabit underwater caves and caverns, guarding these areas and the resources within. Its presence is often associated with dangerous currents, whirlpools, and areas known for drownings. Some stories place its habitat near copper deposits.
behavior: The Water Panther is often depicted as a territorial and unpredictable creature. It can be both benevolent and malevolent, depending on how it is treated and the intentions of those who encounter it. It is said to protect the underwater world and its resources, punishing those who disrespect or exploit them. It is also known to test individuals, rewarding those who demonstrate courage and respect with gifts or guidance. In some stories, it lures people to their deaths, while in others, it acts as a guardian spirit.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Copper
keyMyths.description: The Water Panther is often associated with the origins of copper, guarding the deposits and sometimes granting access to those who prove worthy. Disrespecting the creature or the copper can lead to dire consequences, such as floods or misfortune.

keyMyths.title: The Drowning of the Disrespectful
keyMyths.description: Many stories tell of individuals who disrespected the Water Panther or its domain and were subsequently drowned or met with misfortune. These tales serve as warnings to treat the natural world and its guardians with reverence.
keyMyths.source: Various Great Lakes tribes oral traditions

sources.author: Basil Johnston
sources.description: Provides insights into Ojibwe beliefs and mythology, including the role of Mishipeshu.
sources.text: Provides insights into Ojibwe beliefs and mythology, including the role of Mishipeshu.

sources.author: Thomas Vennum Jr.
sources.work: American Indian Lacrosse: Little Brother of War
sources.description: Discusses the cultural significance of the Water Panther and its connection to various aspects of Native American life.
